name: Ganjifa — Persian & Mughal Cards (the Cousin, not the Parent)
description:
Gorgeous **circular, hand-painted, lacquered** cards whose very name — *ganjifa* / Arabic *kanjifah* — is the **same word** as the Mamluk game. Persia from the 16th century, then richly developed in **Mughal India** (Dashavatara and other sets). **A cousin branch, NOT a parent of tarot** — it shows the Islamic-world card tradition that flourished but did *not* cross into Europe to become the four-suit pack. Filed honestly as adjacent card art, not ancestry.

ANCESTRAL STATUS: **cousin** — beautiful and related, but off the line that became tarot.

SOURCES: LACMA open access (lacma.org); PICRYL public-domain aggregates; Ashmolean Museum.

sections.What it is: Circular lacquered Persian/Mughal cards — the same word as Mamluk *kanjifah*, a stunning cousin of the European pack, not its ancestor.

image_credit: Various collections; reproductions in the public domain
